content:
The body has two main types of glands:

**Endocrine glands:**
• **Ductless** — secrete **hormones** directly into the **bloodstream**.
• Hormones are carried by blood to **target organs** (often far from the gland).
• Act in **very small quantities** but have powerful, widespread effects.
• Examples: pituitary, thyroid, pancreas (islets), adrenal, testes, ovaries.

**Exocrine glands:**
• Have **ducts (tubes)** that carry secretions to a body surface or cavity.
• Secretions usually act **locally** on surfaces.
• Examples: **sweat** glands (skin), **salivary** glands (mouth), **gastric** glands (stomach), **mammary** glands (milk).

**Hormones** — chemical **messengers** produced by endocrine glands; regulate growth, metabolism, reproduction, blood sugar, stress response, etc.

**Must know**
• Endocrine = ductless + into blood.
• Exocrine = with ducts + onto surface/cavity.
• Hormones needed in tiny amounts — 'poured in large quantities' is FALSE.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Hormone — regulatory chemical substance produced by an endocrine gland, transported in blood to stimulate specific target cells.
• Endocrine gland — ductless gland secreting hormones into blood.
• Exocrine gland — gland secreting through a duct onto a surface or into a cavity.

**Exam traps:**
• Pancreas is **mixed** — islets of Langerhans (endocrine: insulin/glucagon) + pancreatic juice ducts (exocrine: digestive enzymes).
• 'Endocrine secretions act on surfaces' describes exocrine, not endocrine.
• Sweat and saliva are NOT hormones.
teacherTip: Endocrine = ENters blood (no Exit duct). Exocrine = EXits through a duct.
examTip: Distinguish exocrine vs endocrine on basis of secretion — mention ducts vs ductless and blood vs surface.

content:
The **pituitary gland** is a **pea-sized** gland at the **base of the brain**, below the hypothalamus. It is called the **'master gland'** because it secretes hormones that **control the activity of other endocrine glands**.

**Key hormones & functions:**
• **Growth Hormone (GH / STH)** — stimulates growth of bones and tissues; promotes overall body growth.
• **TSH** (Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone) — stimulates thyroid to secrete thyroxine.
• **ACTH** — stimulates adrenal cortex.
• **FSH / LH** — regulate reproductive organs (ovaries/testes).

**Disorders linked to GH:**
• **Deficiency in childhood** → dwarfism (stunted growth).
• **Excess in childhood** → gigantism (abnormal height).
• **Excess in adulthood** → acromegaly (enlarged hands, feet, face).

**Must know**
• Called master gland because it **regulates other glands**, not because it is largest or strongest.
• Pituitary also triggers **puberty** by signalling testes/ovaries to produce sex hormones.
• Being pea-sized is true but irrelevant to why it is 'master'.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Master gland — pituitary; controls other endocrine glands via its tropic hormones.
• Growth hormone — pituitary hormone accelerating growth of body cells and tissues.

**Exam traps:**
• 'Pituitary is the largest gland' is FALSE.
• 'Master gland because it is pea-sized' is FALSE — size is not the reason.
• Growth hormone from pituitary, NOT thyroid.
teacherTip: Master = Manager — pituitary manages (controls) the other endocrine glands.
examTip: Give reason: 'Pituitary is master gland' → secretes hormones that regulate activity of other endocrine glands.

content:
**Thyroid gland:**
• **Butterfly-shaped**, bilobed gland on either side of the **windpipe (trachea)**, just below the **larynx**; two lobes joined by the **isthmus**.
• Secretes **thyroxine** (needs **iodine** for its synthesis).
• **Function:** controls **basal metabolic rate** (BMR) — speed of most body chemical reactions; influences growth and development.

**Thyroxine disorders:**
• **Deficiency (hypothyroidism):**
  - In infants → **cretinism** (physical + mental retardation, stunted growth).
  - In adults → **myxoedema** (sluggishness, swelling of face and hands).
  - Iodine deficiency → **simple goitre** (enlarged thyroid).
• **Excess (hyperthyroidism):** increased metabolic rate, weight loss, restlessness, rapid heartbeat — also harmful.

**Parathyroid glands & Parathormone:**
• **Four tiny glands** embedded on the **posterior (back) surface** of thyroid — two on each lobe.
• Secrete **parathormone (PTH)** — regulates **calcium and phosphorus** metabolism in blood and bones.
• Works with thyroid to maintain mineral balance.

**Must know**
• Thyroxine needs **iodine** — 'iodised salt' prevents goitre.
• Oversecretion of thyroxine is harmful, NOT beneficial.
• Parathyroids are ON the thyroid (back), not inside the pituitary.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Thyroxine — thyroid hormone controlling metabolic rate.
• Goitre — enlargement of thyroid due to iodine deficiency.
• Parathormone — hormone from parathyroid glands regulating blood calcium levels.

**Exam traps:**
• Cretinism = infant thyroxine deficiency; Myxoedema = adult thyroxine deficiency.
• 'Oversecretion of thyroxine is very important' is FALSE — it is harmful.
• Parathyroid embedded in **thyroid**, NOT pituitary.
teacherTip: Front view: butterfly + isthmus. Back view: four parathyroids — draw both views for diagram marks.
examTip: Match: Simple goitre→iodine deficiency; Cretinism→infant; Myxoedema→adult; Increased metabolic rate→oversecretion of thyroxine.

content:
The **pancreas** lies **behind the stomach** in the abdomen. It is a **mixed gland** — exocrine (digestive juice via duct) + endocrine (**islets of Langerhans**).

**Insulin** (from beta cells of islets):
• **Lowers blood glucose** — helps cells absorb glucose; converts excess glucose into **glycogen** stored in liver and muscles.
• Deficiency → **diabetes mellitus** (high blood sugar, glucose in urine, thirst, frequent urination).

**Glucagon** (from alpha cells of islets):
• **Raises blood glucose** — stimulates breakdown of **glycogen → glucose** in liver (glycogenolysis).
• Acts when blood sugar is **too low** (e.g. between meals, during fasting).

**Insulin vs Glucagon — antagonistic pair:**
• Insulin ↓ blood sugar; Glucagon ↑ blood sugar.
• Together maintain **blood glucose homeostasis**.

**Must know**
• Insulin from pancreas, NOT thyroid.
• **Insufficient** insulin causes diabetes — NOT oversecretion.
• Insulin converts glucose → glycogen (stores sugar); glucagon does the reverse.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Insulin — pancreatic hormone lowering blood glucose by promoting uptake and glycogen storage.
• Glucagon — pancreatic hormone raising blood glucose by breaking down glycogen.
• Diabetes mellitus — disease caused by insufficient insulin, leading to high blood glucose.

**Exam traps:**
• 'Oversecretion of insulin causes diabetes' is FALSE — deficiency causes it.
• 'Insulin converts glycogen to glucose' is FALSE — that is glucagon's role.
• Glucose → glycogen = insulin; Glycogen → glucose = glucagon.
teacherTip: In-sulin puts sugar IN storage (glycogen). Gluca-gon lets glucose GO out of storage.
examTip: Distinguish insulin vs glucagon on function — opposite effects on blood glucose; give one line each.

content:
Two **adrenal glands**, one on top of each **kidney** (like a cap). Each has two regions:

**Adrenal medulla (inner):**
• Secretes **adrenaline (epinephrine)** — the **'emergency hormone'** / **'fight-or-flight hormone'**.
• Released in large amounts during **stress, fear, anger or excitement**.
• **Effects:** increases heart rate, blood pressure, breathing rate; dilates pupils; redistributes blood to muscles; converts glycogen to glucose for quick energy; reduces digestion.
• Prepares body to **face danger or flee**.

**Adrenal cortex (outer):**
• Secretes **cortisone** (corticosteroid) — influences carbohydrate, fat and protein metabolism; regulates salt and water balance; reduces inflammation.

**Must know**
• Adrenaline from **medulla**, NOT cortex.
• Also called epinephrine — same hormone, two names.
• Secreted in response to emotional/physical stress — 'emergency hormone'.
• Adrenal glands sit above kidneys, NOT in the abdomen near stomach (that's pancreas).
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Adrenaline (epinephrine) — hormone from adrenal medulla preparing the body for emergency response.
• Fight-or-flight response — physiological reaction to perceived threat: increased alertness and energy.

**Exam traps:**
• 'Epinephrine secreted by adrenal cortex' is FALSE — medulla secretes it.
• 'Adrenaline lowers blood glucose' is FALSE — it raises glucose (glycogen breakdown).
• Cortisone = cortex; Adrenaline = medulla — don't swap them.
teacherTip: Medulla = Emergency (adrenaline). Cortex = Cortisone — both C's help remember the cortex hormone.
examTip: Give reason: 'Adrenaline is emergency hormone' → released during stress, prepares body to fight or flee.

content:
**Adolescence:**
• Transitional period between **childhood and adulthood** (roughly **11–19 years**).
• Body undergoes changes leading to **reproductive maturity**.
• Marked by rapid **growth spurt**, emotional changes, and increased self-awareness.

**Puberty:**
• The stage when the **reproductive system matures** and becomes capable of reproduction.
• Triggered by hormones — pituitary signals **testes** (boys) and **ovaries** (girls) to produce sex hormones.

**Changes in boys (testosterone):**
• Facial and body hair growth; **Adam's apple** enlarges; voice **deepens**.
• Penis and testes enlarge; shoulders broaden; muscle development.

**Changes in girls (estrogen):**
• **Breast development**; widening of hips.
• Onset of **menstruation** (monthly cycle); growth of pubic hair.

**Common to both:**
• Growth spurt; acne/pimples; increased sweating; mood swings; development of secondary sexual characters.

**Must know**
• Adolescence is broader than puberty — puberty is part of adolescence.
• Testosterone = male sex hormone; Estrogen = female sex hormone.
• Hormones are responsible for puberty changes — not just growth in general.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Adolescence — period of life between childhood and adulthood with physical and emotional changes toward reproductive maturity.
• Puberty — period when reproductive organs mature and reproduction becomes possible.
• Secondary sexual characters — features distinguishing males and females (not directly involved in reproduction).

**Exam traps:**
• 'Adolescence and puberty are identical' is FALSE — puberty is one phase within adolescence.
• Adam's apple = protruding part of throat due to larynx growth in boys.
• Menstruation begins at puberty in girls — link to estrogen.
teacherTip: Pituitary starts the puberty clock → testes/ovaries release sex hormones → body changes follow.
examTip: List changes separately for boys AND girls PLUS common changes — examiners check all three groups.

content:
Adolescence brings rapid body changes — **physical hygiene** is essential for health, confidence and disease prevention.

**1. Proper & safe food:**
• Balanced diet — proteins, carbohydrates, fats, vitamins, minerals.
• Include milk, fruits, fresh vegetables.
• Avoid stale food, excess fast food/junk food.
• Store perishables in refrigerator.

**2. Proper lifestyle:**
• Avoid long continuous desk work and excessive TV/computer games.
• **Never** smoke, drink alcohol or use drugs — especially harmful during growth years.
• Adequate sleep for growth and hormone balance.

**3. Cleanliness:**
• Wash hands before and after meals.
• Brush teeth twice daily; bathe regularly.
• Change clothes daily — especially **undergarments**.
• Avoid very tight clothing; wear clean, comfortable clothes.
• Wash eyes with clean cold water.
• **Girls:** special care during **menstruation** — use clean sanitary products, change regularly, maintain personal hygiene.

**4. Physical exercise:**
• Walk, exercise, play outdoor games regularly.
• Combats stress and strain of adolescence; builds fitness.

**Must know**
• Hygiene covers diet + lifestyle + cleanliness + exercise — not just bathing.
• Public hygiene (sewage disposal, clean water) is equally important as personal hygiene.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Personal hygiene — practices maintaining cleanliness and health of one's own body.
• Balanced diet — diet containing all nutrients in correct proportions for growth.

**Exam traps:**
• 'Only cleanliness matters in adolescence' is FALSE — all four areas matter.
• Menstrual hygiene is specifically tested for girls — mention sanitary care.
• Junk food excess leads to obesity and nutritional deficiencies during critical growth period.
teacherTip: Four H's: Healthy food, Healthy habits, Hygiene, Exercise (sports).
examTip: Long answer: group points under four headings (food, lifestyle, cleanliness, exercise) — mirrors textbook marking scheme.

content:
**Stress** — feeling of being under too much **mental or emotional pressure**; common during adolescence due to exams, peer pressure, bodily changes and expectations.

**Symptoms of stress:**
• Anxiety, irritability, difficulty concentrating.
• Headaches, disturbed sleep, fatigue.
• Physical effects: increased heart rate (palpitations), sweating.

**Healthy ways to manage stress:**
• **Yoga** — combines physical poses, controlled breathing and meditation; reduces stress, improves flexibility and energy.
• **Exercise** — 30–45 minutes at least 3 times/week (running, swimming, cricket, football); releases feel-good chemicals.
• **Deep breathing & meditation** — calms the mind, lowers heart rate.
• **Proper sleep schedule** — timely, adequate sleep restores body and mind.
• **Reading** — relaxes mind and builds knowledge.
• Talking to trusted adults, friends or counsellors.

**Unhealthy (avoid):**
• Uncontrolled eating, excessive TV, substance abuse — these **worsen** stress.

**Must know**
• Adrenaline is released during stress — links endocrine system to stress response.
• Yoga + exercise + meditation = correct stress management (not uncontrolled eating + TV).
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Stress — body's response to physical, mental or emotional pressure or challenge.
• Stress management — techniques to reduce harmful effects of stress on body and mind.

**Exam traps:**
• 'Watching excessive TV manages stress' is FALSE.
• 'Uncontrolled eating helps stress' is FALSE.
• Palpitations (feeling heart beat hard/fast) are often caused by stress/anxiety — link to ch6 heart conditions.
teacherTip: Good stress relief: Yoga, Exercise, Sleep, Reading. Bad: Junk food binges and screen addiction.
examTip: Name any four stress-busting activities — yoga, exercise, sleep schedule, reading, meditation, deep breathing all score.
